one_line_takeaway: A distortion-free LLM watermark that needs only sampling access -- competitive with or better than white-box schemes on detection, perplexity, and diversity -- but, like its peers, largely defeated by paraphrasing.
key_finding: In the white-box regime (k=1, m=1024) the scheme dominates or matches prior work: perplexity 2.61 with 97.7% detection AUC, versus Aaronson (2.81 PPL, 71.7% raw / 97.9% length-corrected AUC) and Kuditipudi (3.55 PPL, 87.8% AUC), while giving better generation diversity (62.2% best-of-3 win rate vs Aaronson's 45.3%). Matched on perplexity it beats Kirchenbauer (their 2.61 PPL / 97.7% AUC vs Kirchenbauer delta=0.5 at 3.39 PPL / 73.2% AUC). The flat scheme consistently beats its recursive variant (e.g. k=1,m=32: flat 97.8% AUC vs recursive 96.3%). Increasing m improves detection and perplexity but with sharp diminishing returns and reduced diversity; increasing k (fewer watermark-injection opportunities) hurts detection. Entropy and test length T both sharply raise detectability (theory: ~50 test tokens guarantee >=97% ROC-AUC as m grows). Critically for security: paraphrasing drives detection to near-random for most methods, and heavy random-token corruption hurts this scheme and Aaronson's more than Kirchenbauer's or Kuditipudi's.
core_contribution: A watermarking scheme for LLMs that needs only black-box (sampling) access to the model, not white-box access to next-token logits. Each autoregressive step draws m candidate continuations from the LLM, scores each unique candidate's n-grams with a secret-keyed pseudorandom function g(w)=F[h(K|w)], and emits the highest-scoring candidate (argmax over u_i^{m/c_i}, where c_i is the sampled count). The scheme is provably distortion-free (Theorem 4.1: the watermarked output distribution equals the un-watermarked one under a conditional-independence assumption on deduplicated n-gram seeds), and can be chained across successive API providers or nested recursively with multiple secret keys (Algorithm 2, combining per-key p-values via Fisher's method). Detection is framed as a hypothesis test with a length-aware, sum-based p-value p=1-F_{|W|}(sum of R_t). The authors also give a white-box specialization (k=1, where candidate tokens can be sampled from the next-token distribution without extra inference calls) and show it can beat existing white-box schemes. This is the paper's OWN contribution; the closely related WaterMax (Giboulot & Furon 2024) and SynthID (Dathathri et al. 2024) are cited prior work that the paper distinguishes itself from (its scheme is distortion-free where WaterMax is not, and it formally quantifies the role of entropy).
threat_model: Third-party / downstream user of a proprietary LLM API who cannot access logits but wants to watermark and later detect the model's outputs (giving watermarking power to API consumers, since exposing logits is itself a security risk -- cf. Carlini et al. 2024 model-stealing). The adversary possesses watermarked text but NOT the secret key, and tries to evade detection. Two concrete attacks are studied: (1) random token replacement corrupting p in {10,20,30,40,50}% of tokens, and (2) paraphrasing the full watermarked generation with Gemini-1.5-Pro. Detection is a hypothesis test with null H0 = 'not watermarked with our key'; Theorem 4.3 guarantees FPR = 1 - t for threshold t.
limitations: 1) The distortion-free guarantee (Thm 4.1) rests on unique n-grams across sampled sequences being conditionally independent -- 'blatantly violated' when intra-sequence n-grams are strongly correlated, and reusing one key across calls also breaks it; drawing fresh keys would restore it but destroy detectability. 2) Robustness is weak against the realistic paraphrasing attack (detection near-random) -- a known hard problem the paper does not solve. 3) The scheme is more fragile to random-token corruption than Kirchenbauer/Kuditipudi. 4) The recursive multi-key variant underperforms the simple flat scheme, so its value is limited to genuinely multi-party settings. 5) Black-box use with large m can be prohibitively expensive (m API draws per step); the cheaper paraphrase-based candidate generation introduces duplicate n-grams that weaken the signal. 6) Closed-form optimal detection (Thm 4.4) needs a no-duplicate-n-gram assumption that mainly holds for large k / small m; otherwise f1 must be estimated by simulation/KDE. 7) Evaluation is limited to two 7B open models (Mistral-7B, Gemma-7B) rather than the proprietary APIs the black-box motivation targets.

datasets_used: databricks-dolly-15k (5233 prompts from brainstorming/generation/open-QA/summarization categories, human responses >=50 tokens) with Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 (bfloat16) as the main setup
eli5-category test split (4885 examples, prompt = title + selftitle, must contain '?') with Gemma-7B-it (bfloat16) for the completeness/appendix results
C4-realnewslike train split (used only to pre-compute 5000 reference values for the Kuditipudi baseline's permutation test)
quantitative_results: Their flat k=1, m=1024: perplexity 2.61, detection AUC 97.7%, best-of-3 win rate 62.2%
Aaronson baseline: PPL 2.81, raw AUC 71.7%, length-corrected AUC 97.9%, best-of-3 win rate 45.3%
Kuditipudi baseline: PPL 3.55, AUC 87.8%, best-of-3 win rate 67.3%
Kirchenbauer delta=0.5: PPL 3.39, AUC 73.2%; delta=4: PPL 5.81, AUC 99.8% but best-of-3 win rate collapses to 41.2%
Flat vs recursive at k=1,m=32: PPL 3.06 vs 3.29, AUC 97.8% vs 96.3%
Diminishing returns in m (k=1): AUC 90.2% at m=2 -> 95.8% at m=4 -> flattens by m=16
Increasing k hurts detection: at m=32, AUC drops 97.8% (k=1) -> 94.2% (k=50)
Theorem 4.4 example: T=100, k=50, m=64, beta=1 achieves 99.9% TPR at 1% FPR; theory guarantees >=97% ROC-AUC from ~50 test tokens as m grows
Gemma/eli5: k=1,m=1024 gives 1.610 PPL / 93.2% AUC; k=50,m=16 (black-box) gives 1.645 PPL / 89.7% AUC; Kirchenbauer delta=0.5 gives 1.649 PPL / 61.6% AUC
Robustness: k=50,m=32 U(0,1) AUC degrades 94.2% -> 85.5% under 10% random token corruption; paraphrasing drives most methods to near-random detection
baselines_compared: Aaronson Corrected (AC) -- the paper's own length-aware Gamma/chi^2 p-value transform of Aaronson's score
Kirchenbauer et al. (2023a) green-list logit-biasing scheme (KB), n=4, gamma=0.25, delta in {0.5,1,2,3,4}
Kuditipudi et al. (2023) fixed-seed-list scheme with permutation-test detection (K), k=256
